text: Lymph node mapping and anastomosis evaluation by Vizera ELITE II, a novel endoscopic system with infrared fluorescent image during laparoscopic rectal cancer surgery. This is a sixty-year-old male with rectal adenocarcinoma, six centimeters above an averge. The clinical stage was IIIb. We gave him conventional concurrent chemoradiotherapy. The clinical response was good with downsizing and downstaging, so we performed laparoscopic TME twelve weeks later.
The surgeon observes the emergence of fluorescence from the pelvis to the parodic area under infrared mode I, following the pre-surgical injection of ICG into the submucosa layer around the tumor.

The surgeon performs lymph node sampling at the root of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). After confirming no metastasis via frozen section, the IMA and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) are transected. The paralytic lymph node mapping is visible, but radical dissection is not performed due to negative sampling results.

The surgeon transects the rectum and removes the tumor. The colonic stump is retrieved from the umbilical incision. After injecting ICG into the peripheral vein of the left forearm, fluorescent enhancement is observed over the colonic stump. The image system is shifted to mode two to ensure adequate tissue perfusion. Intracorporeal anastomosis is performed, followed by a final check of the anastomosis using ICG fluorescent imaging.
The pathology was stage one with fourteen retrievable negative lymph nodes.
